does: This paper introduces Foresight, a test-time framework for open-world mapless navigation from sparse language instructions that leverages pretrained Vision-Language Models to iteratively reason about task-relevant environmental cues. The core contribution is an alternating procedure where a finetuned VLM proposes image-space motion plans, then critiques them using the language goal and visual context, with subsequent plans conditioned on prior critiques. The framework discovers novel instruction-relevant cues dynamically rather than relying on predetermined navigation factors, and refines motion planning iteratively before execution. Alignment with behavioral preferences is achieved through learning a reward model, enabling the system to handle underspecified goals and identify plan-dependent cues that reveal routes or destinations not yet visible.
connects: This work extends prior navigation research that has typically been constrained by reliance on known navigation factors or closed-set cue categories, such as lane markers, traffic signs, or predetermined semantic classes. It builds on recent advances in Vision-Language Models and their ability to perform zero-shot reasoning about visual content, combining these with iterative refinement approaches common in planning literature. The paper responds to limitations in prior methods that identify environmental cues before motion planning occurs, missing dependencies between routing decisions and which cues become relevant. It also connects to work on language grounding in embodied AI and the challenge of resolving underspecified natural language instructions in complex environments, drawing on developments in open-vocabulary vision and preference learning for aligning model outputs with desired behaviors.
enables: This framework opens new directions for embodied AI systems that can discover task-relevant features online rather than through supervised annotation. It enables more flexible navigation in truly open-world settings where instruction-relevant cues are diverse and unknown a priori, pointing toward systems that reason about why certain observations matter for their goals. Future work could extend iterative refinement to multi-step reasoning about complex spatial reasoning, scaling to longer horizons and more ambiguous instructions. The approach also suggests applications beyond navigation to other embodied decision-making tasks requiring interpretation of underspecified goals and context-dependent cue relevance. Additionally, this work motivates further research into reward learning for aligning iterative reasoning systems with human preferences and into more efficient test-time computation for real-time navigation scenarios.
